carrier_description: Anhydrous ammonia (NH3) with typical purity >99.5% for energy applications. Composed of nitrogen and hydrogen. Requires specialized storage, handling, and transportation infrastructure due to toxicity and reactivity characteristics.

Grid-level electricity transmitted at high voltage (380–220 kV), representing standard transmission infrastructure constraints and voltage level specifications for levels 1–3 of the European electricity grid.
Typically pure CH4 with minimal impurities; used in natural gas infrastructure and combustion processes.

Grid-level electricity distributed at medium voltage (1–36 kV), typically used for residential and commercial supply. Standard purity and quality constraints align with European grid code requirements for distribution networks.

A high-temperature hydrogen-based thermal carrier used in industrial processes, requiring >99.9% hydrogen purity for efficient heat transfer. Typically utilised in catalytic conversion or reforming applications where hydrogen acts as both a heat medium and reactant.
Standard grid-level electricity with typical voltage ranges and infrastructure compatibility.

ThermalBridge refers to a standardized thermal energy carrier used in district heating and cooling networks, characterized by specific temperature ranges (40–130°C) and purity constraints to ensure compatibility with pipeline infrastructure and end-use applications.

Geothermal heat from subsurface reservoirs, typically utilised for low to medium temperature applications such as district heating and industrial process heat. Infrastructure requires drilling and heat extraction systems.

Hydrogen is typically produced via electrolysis, steam methane reforming, or other processes. Standard purity constraints range from 99.9% to 99.999% depending on application. It is stored and transported as compressed gas or liquid, requiring specialized infrastructure to prevent leakage and ensure safety.
